title: AP World History: Modern MCQ Practice Exam 07

questions.prompt: Originalized court poem, Vijayanagara, South India, c. 1400: “The king guards the sacred city where temple towers rise above the market streets. Irrigation channels feed the rice fields, warriors protect the passes, and merchants pay duties on horses, gems, and cloth.” What was the most likely purpose of the poem?

choices.A: To portray the king as protector of religion, agriculture, commerce, and military security
choices.B: To argue that South Indian rulers rejected temple patronage
choices.C: To show that Vijayanagara depended entirely on Atlantic trade
choices.D: To describe the disappearance of urban markets in South Asia

questions.correctAnswerText: To portray the king as protector of religion, agriculture, commerce, and military security
questions.explanation: The poem links the king to temples, irrigation, warriors, markets, and trade duties.

questions.prompt: Originalized court poem, Vijayanagara, South India, c. 1400: “The king guards the sacred city where temple towers rise above the market streets. Irrigation channels feed the rice fields, warriors protect the passes, and merchants pay duties on horses, gems, and cloth.” Which phrase best supports the claim that religious patronage helped legitimize political authority?

choices.A: “Merchants pay duties”
choices.B: “Warriors protect the passes”
choices.C: “Temple towers rise above the market streets”
choices.D: “Horses, gems, and cloth”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Temple towers rise above the market streets”
questions.explanation: The phrase connects religious institutions to the public and political life of the city.

questions.prompt: Originalized court poem, Vijayanagara, South India, c. 1400: “The king guards the sacred city where temple towers rise above the market streets. Irrigation channels feed the rice fields, warriors protect the passes, and merchants pay duties on horses, gems, and cloth.” Which broader environmental context best explains the reference to irrigation channels?

choices.A: South Indian states often invested in water management to support agriculture in monsoon-influenced environments.
choices.B: South Asia lacked agricultural production before 1450.
choices.C: Irrigation was used only in industrial factory towns.
choices.D: Indian Ocean trade made food production unnecessary in South India.

questions.correctAnswerText: South Indian states often invested in water management to support agriculture in monsoon-influenced environments.
questions.explanation: Irrigation channels feeding rice fields reflect environmental management for agrarian production.

questions.prompt: Originalized court poem, Vijayanagara, South India, c. 1400: “The king guards the sacred city where temple towers rise above the market streets. Irrigation channels feed the rice fields, warriors protect the passes, and merchants pay duties on horses, gems, and cloth.” The poem most directly suggests which relationship between state power and commerce?

choices.A: Rulers avoided taxing merchants because trade was considered dishonorable.
choices.B: Commercial activity could help support state revenue and urban life.
choices.C: Trade duties replaced all religious institutions in South Asia.
choices.D: Merchants controlled the army independently of the king.

questions.correctAnswerText: Commercial activity could help support state revenue and urban life.
questions.explanation: Duties on horses, gems, and cloth suggest that trade helped fund and sustain the state.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source description of Polynesian navigation before 1450: “Voyagers used double-hulled canoes, star paths, swell patterns, bird flight, and memorized island chains to settle and maintain contact among widely separated Pacific islands.” Which interpretation is best supported by the description?

choices.A: Oceania was settled only after European ships arrived.
choices.B: Pacific voyagers used specialized maritime knowledge and technology to cross large ocean distances.
choices.C: Polynesian societies rejected all seafaring after 1200.
choices.D: Pacific migration depended on steam-powered ships.

questions.correctAnswerText: Pacific voyagers used specialized maritime knowledge and technology to cross large ocean distances.
questions.explanation: Double-hulled canoes, stars, swell patterns, birds, and memorized island chains show sophisticated maritime technology and knowledge.

questions.prompt:
Table: Selected technologies and knowledge moving across Afro-Eurasia, c. 1200–1450
Item | Earlier major use | Later spread or adaptation
Paper-making | China and Islamic lands | Wider use in Europe and parts of Africa
Mathematical notation | South Asia and Islamic lands | Adapted in Mediterranean scholarship
Gunpowder knowledge | East Asia | Spread westward through Eurasian contacts
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Technologies and knowledge moved across Afro-Eurasian networks and were adapted in new regions.
choices.B: Innovation occurred only in western Europe before 1450.
choices.C: Exchange networks prevented the spread of scientific knowledge.
choices.D: All technologies moved from Europe to Asia.

questions.correctAnswerText: Technologies and knowledge moved across Afro-Eurasian networks and were adapted in new regions.
questions.explanation: The table shows paper-making, mathematical notation, and gunpowder knowledge spreading and being adapted across regions.

questions.prompt:
Table: Selected technologies and knowledge moving across Afro-Eurasia, c. 1200–1450
Item | Earlier major use | Later spread or adaptation
Paper-making | China and Islamic lands | Wider use in Europe and parts of Africa
Mathematical notation | South Asia and Islamic lands | Adapted in Mediterranean scholarship
Gunpowder knowledge | East Asia | Spread westward through Eurasian contacts
Which evidence best supports the claim that Islamic lands served as intermediaries in knowledge transmission?

choices.A: “Gunpowder knowledge | East Asia”
choices.B: “Mathematical notation | South Asia and Islamic lands”
choices.C: “Earlier major use”
choices.D: “Technologies and knowledge”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Mathematical notation | South Asia and Islamic lands”
questions.explanation: This entry directly shows Islamic lands as part of the transmission of mathematical knowledge.

questions.prompt:
Table: Selected technologies and knowledge moving across Afro-Eurasia, c. 1200–1450
Item | Earlier major use | Later spread or adaptation
Paper-making | China and Islamic lands | Wider use in Europe and parts of Africa
Mathematical notation | South Asia and Islamic lands | Adapted in Mediterranean scholarship
Gunpowder knowledge | East Asia | Spread westward through Eurasian contacts
Which broader context best explains the pattern shown in the table?

choices.A: The decline of all long-distance trade after 1200
choices.B: The growth of interconnected trade routes, scholarly centers, and imperial networks across Afro-Eurasia
choices.C: The isolation of the Mediterranean from Asia
choices.D: The replacement of manuscript culture by radio communication

questions.correctAnswerText: The growth of interconnected trade routes, scholarly centers, and imperial networks across Afro-Eurasia
questions.explanation: Trade, scholarship, and imperial connections helped move technologies and ideas across Afro-Eurasia.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the Strait of Malacca c. 1400 shows narrow sea lanes between Sumatra and the Malay Peninsula. Arrows mark ships carrying spices, ceramics, textiles, and forest products between the South China Sea and the Indian Ocean. Port symbols cluster at river mouths.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Geographic chokepoints could make Southeast Asian ports important centers of exchange.
choices.B: Southeast Asian ports were disconnected from Indian Ocean trade.
choices.C: Forest products moved only by land caravan routes.
choices.D: The South China Sea had no connection to the Indian Ocean.

questions.correctAnswerText: Geographic chokepoints could make Southeast Asian ports important centers of exchange.
questions.explanation: The narrow Strait of Malacca linked the South China Sea and Indian Ocean, making port control valuable.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the Strait of Malacca c. 1400 shows narrow sea lanes between Sumatra and the Malay Peninsula. Arrows mark ships carrying spices, ceramics, textiles, and forest products between the South China Sea and the Indian Ocean. Port symbols cluster at river mouths.” A ruler seeking to control the routes shown would most likely try to do which of the following?

choices.A: Abandon port cities and move all subjects inland
choices.B: Tax shipping, protect harbors, and negotiate with merchant communities
choices.C: Ban all foreign merchants from Southeast Asia
choices.D: Replace maritime trade with Atlantic plantation labor

questions.correctAnswerText: Tax shipping, protect harbors, and negotiate with merchant communities
questions.explanation: Controlling chokepoints required taxation, harbor security, and cooperation with merchants.

questions.prompt: Originalized Saadi Moroccan decree, late sixteenth century: “The sharifian ruler commands that musketeers be paid from the treasury and that caravan duties be counted at the desert towns. Judges shall proclaim the ruler’s descent from the Prophet, and envoys shall report on gold routes beyond the Sahara.” What was the most likely purpose of this decree?

choices.A: To coordinate military reform, religious legitimacy, and control over trans-Saharan revenue
choices.B: To abolish all Islamic legal institutions in Morocco
choices.C: To make Morocco dependent on Portuguese colonial officials
choices.D: To end all contact between Morocco and the Sahara

questions.correctAnswerText: To coordinate military reform, religious legitimacy, and control over trans-Saharan revenue
questions.explanation: The decree links musketeers, treasury payments, caravan duties, religious lineage, and Saharan gold routes.

questions.prompt: Originalized Saadi Moroccan decree, late sixteenth century: “The sharifian ruler commands that musketeers be paid from the treasury and that caravan duties be counted at the desert towns. Judges shall proclaim the ruler’s descent from the Prophet, and envoys shall report on gold routes beyond the Sahara.” Which phrase best supports the claim that religious lineage helped legitimize rule?

choices.A: “Musketeers be paid”
choices.B: “Caravan duties”
choices.C: “Gold routes”
choices.D: “Descent from the Prophet”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Descent from the Prophet”
questions.explanation: Claiming descent from the Prophet provided religious legitimacy for the ruler.

questions.prompt: Originalized Saadi Moroccan decree, late sixteenth century: “The sharifian ruler commands that musketeers be paid from the treasury and that caravan duties be counted at the desert towns. Judges shall proclaim the ruler’s descent from the Prophet, and envoys shall report on gold routes beyond the Sahara.” The reference to musketeers most directly reflects which broader early modern development?

choices.A: The use of gunpowder weapons by states seeking to expand or defend authority
choices.B: The decline of firearms in land-based empires after 1450
choices.C: The replacement of armies by elected parliaments
choices.D: The disappearance of cavalry and infantry from warfare

questions.correctAnswerText: The use of gunpowder weapons by states seeking to expand or defend authority
questions.explanation: Musketeers indicate the use of gunpowder military technology in early modern state-building.

questions.prompt: Originalized Saadi Moroccan decree, late sixteenth century: “The sharifian ruler commands that musketeers be paid from the treasury and that caravan duties be counted at the desert towns. Judges shall proclaim the ruler’s descent from the Prophet, and envoys shall report on gold routes beyond the Sahara.” Which argument is best supported by the decree?

choices.A: Moroccan rulers avoided using commerce to support state power.
choices.B: Early modern state-building could combine military technology, sacred legitimacy, and control of trade revenue.
choices.C: Desert commerce became irrelevant after gunpowder spread.
choices.D: Religious legitimacy prevented rulers from maintaining armies.

questions.correctAnswerText: Early modern state-building could combine military technology, sacred legitimacy, and control of trade revenue.
questions.explanation: The decree combines musketeers, prophetic descent, and caravan duties.

questions.prompt: Described royal law tablet, Ayutthaya, Siam, seventeenth century: “A carved panel shows the king seated above ranked officials, monks, and provincial chiefs. An inscription orders labor service for canal repair, military duty when called, and tribute from rice-growing districts.” Which interpretation is best supported by the object?

choices.A: Ayutthaya’s state authority rested only on overseas merchants.
choices.B: The ruler claimed authority over religious, administrative, military, and labor hierarchies.
choices.C: Siamese rulers rejected Buddhist symbols in political display.
choices.D: Provincial chiefs governed independently of the king.

questions.correctAnswerText: The ruler claimed authority over religious, administrative, military, and labor hierarchies.
questions.explanation: The panel shows the king above officials, monks, chiefs, labor service, military duty, and tribute.

questions.prompt: Described royal law tablet, Ayutthaya, Siam, seventeenth century: “A carved panel shows the king seated above ranked officials, monks, and provincial chiefs. An inscription orders labor service for canal repair, military duty when called, and tribute from rice-growing districts.” Which phrase best supports the claim that the state organized social obligations?

choices.A: “King seated above”
choices.B: “Labor service for canal repair”
choices.C: “Carved panel”
choices.D: “Rice-growing districts”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Labor service for canal repair”
questions.explanation: Labor service shows the state organizing social obligations for public works.

questions.prompt: Described royal law tablet, Ayutthaya, Siam, seventeenth century: “A carved panel shows the king seated above ranked officials, monks, and provincial chiefs. An inscription orders labor service for canal repair, military duty when called, and tribute from rice-growing districts.” Which broader context best explains the reference to canal repair and rice districts?

choices.A: Southeast Asian states often linked political power to control of wet-rice agriculture and water systems.
choices.B: Ayutthaya depended on Arctic hunting routes.
choices.C: Rice cultivation disappeared from mainland Southeast Asia after 1450.
choices.D: Canal repair was unrelated to taxation or state authority.

questions.correctAnswerText: Southeast Asian states often linked political power to control of wet-rice agriculture and water systems.
questions.explanation: Canal repair and rice tribute show the connection between state authority, water control, and agriculture.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the Cape of Good Hope c. 1650–1720 shows a fortified company settlement, gardens supplying passing ships, cattle-trade routes with Khoikhoi communities, and sea lanes connecting the Atlantic and Indian Oceans.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: The Cape became a strategic provisioning point linking Atlantic and Indian Ocean routes.
choices.B: The Cape was isolated from maritime trade after 1650.
choices.C: Khoikhoi communities had no contact with company settlements.
choices.D: European ships avoided the southern African coast.

questions.correctAnswerText: The Cape became a strategic provisioning point linking Atlantic and Indian Ocean routes.
questions.explanation: The map shows a company settlement, gardens, cattle routes, and sea lanes between two oceanic systems.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the Cape of Good Hope c. 1650–1720 shows a fortified company settlement, gardens supplying passing ships, cattle-trade routes with Khoikhoi communities, and sea lanes connecting the Atlantic and Indian Oceans.” Which feature best supports the claim that the settlement served maritime commerce?

choices.A: “Gardens supplying passing ships”
choices.B: “Khoikhoi communities”
choices.C: “Southern African coast”
choices.D: “Fortified company settlement”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Gardens supplying passing ships”
questions.explanation: Supplying ships shows the Cape’s function as a provisioning station for maritime commerce.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the Cape of Good Hope c. 1650–1720 shows a fortified company settlement, gardens supplying passing ships, cattle-trade routes with Khoikhoi communities, and sea lanes connecting the Atlantic and Indian Oceans.” Which broader process best explains the company settlement shown?

choices.A: Chartered European companies established strategic posts to support long-distance oceanic trade.
choices.B: African states ended all trade with Europeans after 1500.
choices.C: The Cape served as the capital of a land-based Asian empire.
choices.D: European merchants abandoned fortified posts after 1450.

questions.correctAnswerText: Chartered European companies established strategic posts to support long-distance oceanic trade.
questions.explanation: The fortified company settlement helped support ships moving between the Atlantic and Indian Oceans.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the Cape of Good Hope c. 1650–1720 shows a fortified company settlement, gardens supplying passing ships, cattle-trade routes with Khoikhoi communities, and sea lanes connecting the Atlantic and Indian Oceans.” Which argument is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Transoceanic commerce reshaped local relationships at strategic coastal nodes.
choices.B: The Cape settlement existed only for missionary activity.
choices.C: Oceanic trade had no effect on African coastal societies.
choices.D: The Cape route connected only European ports to one another.

questions.correctAnswerText: Transoceanic commerce reshaped local relationships at strategic coastal nodes.
questions.explanation: The map shows company settlement, ship provisioning, sea lanes, and cattle trade with local communities.

questions.prompt: Described casta painting, New Spain, eighteenth century: “A Spanish man, an Indigenous woman, and their child stand in a domestic interior. Labels identify ancestry, clothing marks status differences, and imported porcelain sits beside local woven goods.” Which interpretation is best supported by the painting?

choices.A: Colonial society in New Spain represented ancestry and status through visual categories and material culture.
choices.B: Colonial Spanish America had no concern with social hierarchy.
choices.C: The painting shows a society untouched by transoceanic exchange.
choices.D: Indigenous people controlled all legal categories in New Spain.

questions.correctAnswerText: Colonial society in New Spain represented ancestry and status through visual categories and material culture.
questions.explanation: Labels, clothing, ancestry, porcelain, and local goods all point to colonial status classification.

questions.prompt: Described casta painting, New Spain, eighteenth century: “A Spanish man, an Indigenous woman, and their child stand in a domestic interior. Labels identify ancestry, clothing marks status differences, and imported porcelain sits beside local woven goods.” Which detail best supports the claim that colonial culture blended local and global goods?

choices.A: The domestic interior
choices.B: The ancestry labels
choices.C: Imported porcelain beside local woven goods
choices.D: The Spanish man

questions.correctAnswerText: Imported porcelain beside local woven goods
questions.explanation: Imported and local goods together show material blending in colonial culture.

questions.prompt: Described casta painting, New Spain, eighteenth century: “A Spanish man, an Indigenous woman, and their child stand in a domestic interior. Labels identify ancestry, clothing marks status differences, and imported porcelain sits beside local woven goods.” Which limitation should a historian recognize when using this painting?

choices.A: It can reveal elite colonial ideas about classification, but it may not accurately represent lived experiences of all mixed-ancestry people.
choices.B: It proves that colonial hierarchy did not exist.
choices.C: It is useless because visual sources cannot show social values.
choices.D: It describes only industrial labor conditions after 1900.

questions.correctAnswerText: It can reveal elite colonial ideas about classification, but it may not accurately represent lived experiences of all mixed-ancestry people.
questions.explanation: Casta paintings are valuable for studying elite classification, but they are limited as evidence for everyday lived experience.

questions.prompt: Originalized Siamese royal reform decree, c. 1895: “Officials shall be salaried by the treasury, not by hidden fees. Schools shall train clerks in numbers, maps, and foreign languages. Corvée obligations will be reduced so that the kingdom may be strong without disorder.” What was the most likely purpose of the decree?

choices.A: To strengthen the state through administrative, educational, and labor reforms
choices.B: To invite direct colonial rule over Siam
choices.C: To abolish all schools and written administration
choices.D: To restore hereditary rule by foreign merchants

questions.correctAnswerText: To strengthen the state through administrative, educational, and labor reforms
questions.explanation: Salaried officials, clerk training, and reduced corvée obligations reflect state-strengthening reforms.

questions.prompt: Originalized Siamese royal reform decree, c. 1895: “Officials shall be salaried by the treasury, not by hidden fees. Schools shall train clerks in numbers, maps, and foreign languages. Corvée obligations will be reduced so that the kingdom may be strong without disorder.” Which phrase best supports the claim that the decree promoted technical and bureaucratic education?

choices.A: “Hidden fees”
choices.B: “Train clerks in numbers, maps, and foreign languages”
choices.C: “Corvée obligations”
choices.D: “Without disorder”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Train clerks in numbers, maps, and foreign languages”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly shows technical and bureaucratic education.

questions.prompt: Originalized Siamese royal reform decree, c. 1895: “Officials shall be salaried by the treasury, not by hidden fees. Schools shall train clerks in numbers, maps, and foreign languages. Corvée obligations will be reduced so that the kingdom may be strong without disorder.” Which broader context best explains the reforms described?

choices.A: Siamese rulers pursued modernization to preserve independence amid European imperial pressure in Southeast Asia.
choices.B: Siam had already become an overseas colony of Spain.
choices.C: Southeast Asian rulers rejected all administrative reform after 1750.
choices.D: Corvée labor expanded because industrial factories disappeared.

questions.correctAnswerText: Siamese rulers pursued modernization to preserve independence amid European imperial pressure in Southeast Asia.
questions.explanation: Siam’s reforms were partly intended to strengthen the state and avoid direct colonization.

questions.prompt: Originalized Siamese royal reform decree, c. 1895: “Officials shall be salaried by the treasury, not by hidden fees. Schools shall train clerks in numbers, maps, and foreign languages. Corvée obligations will be reduced so that the kingdom may be strong without disorder.” Which statement best evaluates the decree’s argument?

choices.A: It claims that reform must destroy the monarchy immediately.
choices.B: It argues that state centralization and selective modernization can strengthen sovereignty.
choices.C: It rejects bureaucratic salaries as unnecessary.
choices.D: It defines modernization as the abandonment of all local authority to foreign powers.

questions.correctAnswerText: It argues that state centralization and selective modernization can strengthen sovereignty.
questions.explanation: The decree links administrative reform, education, labor reduction, and national strength.

questions.prompt: Originalized women’s rights petition, France, 1791: “The nation declares rights in the name of reason, yet wives remain minors before the law and daughters are educated for obedience. If citizenship rests on nature and reason, women must not be citizens only when taxes are collected.” Which audience was the petition most likely intended to influence?

choices.A: Revolutionary legislators and readers debating the meaning of citizenship
choices.B: Qing officials regulating foreign trade at Guangzhou
choices.C: Mongol commanders organizing cavalry raids
choices.D: Colonial planters defending plantation slavery

questions.correctAnswerText: Revolutionary legislators and readers debating the meaning of citizenship
questions.explanation: The petition addresses rights, citizenship, law, education, and taxation in revolutionary France.

questions.prompt: Originalized women’s rights petition, France, 1791: “The nation declares rights in the name of reason, yet wives remain minors before the law and daughters are educated for obedience. If citizenship rests on nature and reason, women must not be citizens only when taxes are collected.” Which phrase best supports the claim that the petition criticized unequal legal status?

choices.A: “Rights in the name of reason”
choices.B: “Wives remain minors before the law”
choices.C: “Taxes are collected”
choices.D: “Daughters are educated”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Wives remain minors before the law”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly criticizes women’s unequal legal status.

questions.prompt: Originalized women’s rights petition, France, 1791: “The nation declares rights in the name of reason, yet wives remain minors before the law and daughters are educated for obedience. If citizenship rests on nature and reason, women must not be citizens only when taxes are collected.” The petition most directly reflects which broader ideological development?

choices.A: The use of Enlightenment language to challenge gender hierarchy
choices.B: The rejection of all natural-rights arguments by reformers
choices.C: The defense of patriarchal law as revolutionary equality
choices.D: The decline of public political debate during the French Revolution

questions.correctAnswerText: The use of Enlightenment language to challenge gender hierarchy
questions.explanation: The petition applies reason, nature, rights, and citizenship to challenge women’s exclusion.

questions.prompt: Originalized women’s rights petition, France, 1791: “The nation declares rights in the name of reason, yet wives remain minors before the law and daughters are educated for obedience. If citizenship rests on nature and reason, women must not be citizens only when taxes are collected.” Which statement best evaluates the petition’s argument?

choices.A: It argues that taxation justifies excluding women from citizenship.
choices.B: It accepts that revolutionary rights should apply only to men.
choices.C: It exposes a contradiction between universal rights language and women’s legal exclusion.
choices.D: It rejects education as a path to political equality.

questions.correctAnswerText: It exposes a contradiction between universal rights language and women’s legal exclusion.
questions.explanation: The petition argues that universal rights language conflicts with women’s limited legal and civic status.

questions.prompt:
Table: Dutch Cultivation System in Java, simplified pattern
Policy | Reported result
Villages required to plant export crops | Coffee and sugar output rose
Colonial officials rewarded for quotas | Pressure on village labor increased
Food land diverted in some areas | Local shortages and peasant grievances grew
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Colonial policies could reorganize agriculture to serve export production.
choices.B: Java avoided participation in global commodity markets.
choices.C: Export crops reduced pressure on village labor.
choices.D: Colonial officials opposed all quota systems.

questions.correctAnswerText: Colonial policies could reorganize agriculture to serve export production.
questions.explanation: Villages were required to plant export crops, increasing coffee and sugar output.

questions.prompt:
Table: Dutch Cultivation System in Java, simplified pattern
Policy | Reported result
Villages required to plant export crops | Coffee and sugar output rose
Colonial officials rewarded for quotas | Pressure on village labor increased
Food land diverted in some areas | Local shortages and peasant grievances grew
Which evidence best supports the claim that ordinary villagers bore costs of the system?

choices.A: “Coffee and sugar output rose”
choices.B: “Colonial officials rewarded”
choices.C: “Pressure on village labor increased”
choices.D: “Export crops”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Pressure on village labor increased”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly indicates the burden placed on ordinary villagers.

questions.prompt:
Table: Dutch Cultivation System in Java, simplified pattern
Policy | Reported result
Villages required to plant export crops | Coffee and sugar output rose
Colonial officials rewarded for quotas | Pressure on village labor increased
Food land diverted in some areas | Local shortages and peasant grievances grew
Which broader environmental and economic process best explains the pattern shown?

choices.A: The conversion of land and labor toward cash crops for industrial-era global markets
choices.B: The disappearance of tropical export crops after 1750
choices.C: The replacement of colonial agriculture by pastoral nomadism
choices.D: The end of food shortages under imperial rule

questions.correctAnswerText: The conversion of land and labor toward cash crops for industrial-era global markets
questions.explanation: The system diverted land and labor to export crops like coffee and sugar.

questions.prompt:
Table: Dutch Cultivation System in Java, simplified pattern
Policy | Reported result
Villages required to plant export crops | Coffee and sugar output rose
Colonial officials rewarded for quotas | Pressure on village labor increased
Food land diverted in some areas | Local shortages and peasant grievances grew
Which argument is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Colonial extraction could increase export revenue while intensifying local social and environmental stress.
choices.B: Colonial governments avoided intervention in village agriculture.
choices.C: Peasant grievances disappeared when export production increased.
choices.D: Export agriculture eliminated the need for colonial officials.

questions.correctAnswerText: Colonial extraction could increase export revenue while intensifying local social and environmental stress.
questions.explanation: The table shows export growth alongside labor pressure, food land diversion, shortages, and grievances.

questions.prompt: Described imperial exhibition poster, London, c. 1890: “A map places Britain at the center with colored routes leading to India, Africa, and the Pacific. Figures labeled ‘commerce,’ ‘science,’ and ‘civilization’ stand above smaller colonized figures carrying raw materials. The caption reads, ‘Progress follows the flag.’” Which interpretation is best supported by the poster?

choices.A: Imperial culture often justified empire by linking conquest to commerce, science, and claims of civilization.
choices.B: The poster criticizes imperialism from an anti-colonial perspective.
choices.C: British imperialism rejected visual propaganda.
choices.D: The poster argues that colonies controlled Britain’s economy.

questions.correctAnswerText: Imperial culture often justified empire by linking conquest to commerce, science, and claims of civilization.
questions.explanation: The poster links imperial routes to commerce, science, civilization, raw materials, and progress.

questions.prompt: Described imperial exhibition poster, London, c. 1890: “A map places Britain at the center with colored routes leading to India, Africa, and the Pacific. Figures labeled ‘commerce,’ ‘science,’ and ‘civilization’ stand above smaller colonized figures carrying raw materials. The caption reads, ‘Progress follows the flag.’” Which detail most directly supports the claim that empire was linked to resource extraction?

choices.A: “Colored routes”
choices.B: “London, c. 1890”
choices.C: “Colonized figures carrying raw materials”
choices.D: “Science”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Colonized figures carrying raw materials”
questions.explanation: This detail directly connects empire to the extraction of raw materials.

questions.prompt: Described imperial exhibition poster, London, c. 1890: “A map places Britain at the center with colored routes leading to India, Africa, and the Pacific. Figures labeled ‘commerce,’ ‘science,’ and ‘civilization’ stand above smaller colonized figures carrying raw materials. The caption reads, ‘Progress follows the flag.’” Which audience was the poster most likely designed to influence?

choices.A: British visitors and consumers at an imperial exhibition
choices.B: Mongol caravan merchants in Central Asia
choices.C: Enslaved workers negotiating maroon autonomy
choices.D: Postcolonial delegates at the United Nations

questions.correctAnswerText: British visitors and consumers at an imperial exhibition
questions.explanation: The poster was designed to shape public support among exhibition visitors in Britain.

questions.prompt: Described imperial exhibition poster, London, c. 1890: “A map places Britain at the center with colored routes leading to India, Africa, and the Pacific. Figures labeled ‘commerce,’ ‘science,’ and ‘civilization’ stand above smaller colonized figures carrying raw materials. The caption reads, ‘Progress follows the flag.’” Which statement best evaluates the poster’s argument?

choices.A: It presents imperial hierarchy as benevolent progress while minimizing coercion and colonial inequality.
choices.B: It argues that colonized peoples should control British policy.
choices.C: It rejects commerce as a motive for empire.
choices.D: It presents imperialism as a purely local British issue unrelated to global power.

questions.correctAnswerText: It presents imperial hierarchy as benevolent progress while minimizing coercion and colonial inequality.
questions.explanation: The poster portrays empire as progress while depicting colonized peoples as subordinate resource bearers.

questions.prompt:
Table: Naval blockade and food imports during World War I, simplified pattern
Region | Prewar import dependence | Wartime disruption
Germany | High dependence on grain and fertilizer imports | Severe shortages by 1917
Austria-Hungary | Dependent on internal rail and regional food flows | Urban rationing and black markets
Ottoman eastern provinces | Vulnerable transport and harvest systems | Local famine conditions
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: World War I disrupted food systems and civilian economies as well as armies.
choices.B: World War I had no effect on civilian life.
choices.C: Food shortages were limited to one empire.
choices.D: Blockades increased agricultural imports for all belligerents.

questions.correctAnswerText: World War I disrupted food systems and civilian economies as well as armies.
questions.explanation: The table shows shortages, rationing, black markets, and famine conditions.

questions.prompt:
Table: Naval blockade and food imports during World War I, simplified pattern
Region | Prewar import dependence | Wartime disruption
Germany | High dependence on grain and fertilizer imports | Severe shortages by 1917
Austria-Hungary | Dependent on internal rail and regional food flows | Urban rationing and black markets
Ottoman eastern provinces | Vulnerable transport and harvest systems | Local famine conditions
Which broader feature of total war is most directly reflected in the table?

choices.A: Military conflict could weaponize access to food, transport, and resources.
choices.B: Civilian economies became independent of military strategy.
choices.C: States avoided controlling food distribution during war.
choices.D: Environmental conditions became irrelevant to wartime outcomes.

questions.correctAnswerText: Military conflict could weaponize access to food, transport, and resources.
questions.explanation: Blockades and disruptions of transport and imports show food and resources becoming tools of war.

questions.prompt: Originalized United Nations field report, Rwanda, 1994: “Radio messages named neighbors as enemies, roadblocks checked identity cards, and families fled to churches and schools seeking safety. Peacekeepers report that killing spread through local officials, militias, and fear.” What was the most likely purpose of the report?

choices.A: To document mechanisms of mass violence and warn international officials
choices.B: To prove that mass violence occurred only between armies
choices.C: To praise radio broadcasts as neutral entertainment
choices.D: To argue that local officials had no role in violence

questions.correctAnswerText: To document mechanisms of mass violence and warn international officials
questions.explanation: The report identifies radio propaganda, identity cards, roadblocks, militias, officials, and civilian flight.

questions.prompt: Originalized United Nations field report, Rwanda, 1994: “Radio messages named neighbors as enemies, roadblocks checked identity cards, and families fled to churches and schools seeking safety. Peacekeepers report that killing spread through local officials, militias, and fear.” Which phrase best supports the claim that media technology helped intensify violence?

choices.A: “Roadblocks checked identity cards”
choices.B: “Families fled”
choices.C: “Radio messages named neighbors as enemies”
choices.D: “Local officials”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Radio messages named neighbors as enemies”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly shows media being used to intensify violence.

questions.prompt: Originalized United Nations field report, Rwanda, 1994: “Radio messages named neighbors as enemies, roadblocks checked identity cards, and families fled to churches and schools seeking safety. Peacekeepers report that killing spread through local officials, militias, and fear.” Which statement best evaluates the report’s argument?

choices.A: It shows that genocide can involve propaganda, identity classification, local institutions, and militia violence.
choices.B: It claims that genocide occurs only through industrial bombing.
choices.C: It denies that civilians were targeted.
choices.D: It argues that international institutions always prevented mass violence.

questions.correctAnswerText: It shows that genocide can involve propaganda, identity classification, local institutions, and militia violence.
questions.explanation: The report links radio propaganda, identity checks, local officials, militias, and civilians seeking refuge.

questions.prompt: Originalized Vietnamese declaration, 1945: “For decades our rice, mines, and taxes served another flag. Now the people declare that independence must bring schools, land reform, and freedom from hunger. We ask the world to recognize that a nation cannot be ruled forever by force.” What was the most likely purpose of the declaration?

choices.A: To justify renewed colonial rule in Vietnam
choices.B: To claim national independence and link it to social reform
choices.C: To reject education and land reform after independence
choices.D: To support permanent foreign military occupation

questions.correctAnswerText: To claim national independence and link it to social reform
questions.explanation: The declaration calls for independence along with schools, land reform, and freedom from hunger.

questions.prompt: Originalized Vietnamese declaration, 1945: “For decades our rice, mines, and taxes served another flag. Now the people declare that independence must bring schools, land reform, and freedom from hunger. We ask the world to recognize that a nation cannot be ruled forever by force.” Which phrase best supports the claim that colonial rule involved economic extraction?

choices.A: “Served another flag”
choices.B: “Our rice, mines, and taxes”
choices.C: “The people declare”
choices.D: “Recognize”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Our rice, mines, and taxes”
questions.explanation: Rice, mines, and taxes directly identify economic resources extracted under colonial rule.

questions.prompt: Originalized Vietnamese declaration, 1945: “For decades our rice, mines, and taxes served another flag. Now the people declare that independence must bring schools, land reform, and freedom from hunger. We ask the world to recognize that a nation cannot be ruled forever by force.” Which broader context best explains the declaration?

choices.A: The post-World War II growth of anticolonial nationalism in Asia
choices.B: The collapse of all nationalist movements after 1919
choices.C: The creation of maritime spice monopolies in the seventeenth century
choices.D: The rise of European feudalism after 1450

questions.correctAnswerText: The post-World War II growth of anticolonial nationalism in Asia
questions.explanation: The 1945 declaration fits the postwar surge of anticolonial claims in Asia.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Angola, c. 1975–1988, marks three rival movements controlling different regions, Soviet and Cuban support flowing to one side, United States and South African support flowing to another, and oil and diamond zones near contested areas.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Decolonization and Cold War rivalries could combine in proxy conflicts over territory and resources.
choices.B: Angola’s independence ended all foreign involvement immediately.
choices.C: The Cold War had no influence in Africa.
choices.D: Natural resources were absent from Angola’s conflict.

questions.correctAnswerText: Decolonization and Cold War rivalries could combine in proxy conflicts over territory and resources.
questions.explanation: The map shows rival movements, foreign Cold War support, and contested oil and diamond zones.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Angola, c. 1975–1988, marks three rival movements controlling different regions, Soviet and Cuban support flowing to one side, United States and South African support flowing to another, and oil and diamond zones near contested areas.” The map most directly reflects which broader pattern?

choices.A: The disappearance of ideological conflict after decolonization
choices.B: The use of newly independent states as arenas for competing Cold War alliances and development models
choices.C: The end of resource politics in postcolonial Africa
choices.D: The replacement of all African political parties by European monarchies

questions.correctAnswerText: The use of newly independent states as arenas for competing Cold War alliances and development models
questions.explanation: Angola’s civil conflict involved rival movements, foreign backing, and competing political-economic visions.

questions.prompt:
Table: Global internet connectivity, simplified pattern
Indicator | c. 1995 | c. 2020
Estimated internet users | Fewer than 50 million | More than 4 billion
Major data routes | Concentrated in North America, Europe, and East Asia | Expanded undersea cable routes across most ocean basins
Online services | Email and basic web pages | Streaming, mobile apps, cloud services, e-commerce
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Digital networks expanded dramatically and became central to global communication and commerce.
choices.B: Internet use declined after 1995.
choices.C: Undersea cable routes disappeared after mobile phones spread.
choices.D: Online services remained unchanged from 1995 to 2020.

questions.correctAnswerText: Digital networks expanded dramatically and became central to global communication and commerce.
questions.explanation: Internet users increased from fewer than 50 million to more than 4 billion, while services and cable routes expanded.

questions.prompt:
Table: Global internet connectivity, simplified pattern
Indicator | c. 1995 | c. 2020
Estimated internet users | Fewer than 50 million | More than 4 billion
Major data routes | Concentrated in North America, Europe, and East Asia | Expanded undersea cable routes across most ocean basins

The pattern shown most directly contributed to which development?

choices.A: The end of transnational business activity
choices.B: The expansion of digital markets, remote services, and platform-based commerce
choices.C: The disappearance of information inequality
choices.D: The return to preindustrial communication systems

questions.correctAnswerText: The expansion of digital markets, remote services, and platform-based commerce
questions.explanation: Streaming, mobile apps, cloud services, and e-commerce indicate new digital economic systems.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation of digital globalization: “The internet made services, entertainment, and finance more mobile, but its material base remained physical: data centers consumed electricity, cables crossed ocean floors, and discarded devices created new waste streams.” Which statement best evaluates this interpretation?

choices.A: It argues that digital globalization had no environmental effects.
choices.B: It shows that digital networks combined virtual exchange with physical infrastructure and environmental costs.
choices.C: It claims that online services ended global inequality.
choices.D: It denies that technology shaped globalization after 1995.

questions.correctAnswerText: It shows that digital networks combined virtual exchange with physical infrastructure and environmental costs.
questions.explanation: The interpretation highlights data centers, electricity use, undersea cables, discarded devices, and digital exchange.
